source: CIS GOOGLE KUBERNETES ENGINE (GKE) BENCHMARK V1.7.0 PDF
title: Ensure Legacy Authorization (ABAC) is Disabled

input_row.source: CIS GOOGLE KUBERNETES ENGINE (GKE) BENCHMARK V1.7.0 PDF
input_row.title: Ensure Legacy Authorization (ABAC) is Disabled
input_row.description: Legacy Authorization, also known as Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) has been superseded by Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) and is not under active development. RBAC is the recommended way to manage permissions in Kubernetes.
input_row.rationale: In Kubernetes, RBAC is used to grant permissions to resources at the cluster and namespace level. RBAC allows the definition of roles with rules containing a set of permissions, whilst the legacy authorizer (ABAC) in Kubernetes Engine grants broad, statically defined permissions. As RBAC provides significant security advantages over ABAC, it is recommended option for access control. Where possible, legacy authorization must be disabled for GKE clusters. Impact: Once the cluster has the legacy authorizer disabled, the user must be granted the ability to create authorization roles using RBAC to ensure that the role-based access control permissions take effect.
input_row.audit: Using Google Cloud Console: 1. Go to Kubernetes Engine by visiting: https://console.cloud.google.com/kubernetes/list. 2. From the list of clusters, click on each cluster to open the Details pane, and make sure 'Legacy Authorization' is set to 'Disabled'. Using Command Line: To check Legacy Authorization status for an existing cluster, run the following command: gcloud container clusters describe <cluster_name> --zone <compute_zone> -- format json | jq '.legacyAbac' The output should return null ({}) if Legacy Authorization is Disabled. If Legacy Authorization is Enabled, the above command will return true value.
input_row.remediation: Using Google Cloud Console: 1. Go to Kubernetes Engine by visiting: https://console.cloud.google.com/kubernetes/list. 2. Select Kubernetes clusters for which Legacy Authorization is enabled. 3. Click EDIT. 4. Set 'Legacy Authorization' to 'Disabled'. 5. Click SAVE. Using Command Line: To disable Legacy Authorization for an existing cluster, run the following command: gcloud container clusters update <cluster_name> --zone <compute_zone> --no- enable-legacy-authorization Default Value: Kubernetes Engine clusters running GKE version 1.8 and later disable the legacy authorization system by default, and thus role-based access control permissions take effect with no special action required. References: 1. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/role-based-access- control 2. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/hardening-your- cluster#leave_abac_disabled_default_for_110 Additional Information: On clusters running GKE 1.6 or 1.7, Kubernetes Service accounts have full permissions on the Kubernetes API by default. To ensure that the role-based access control permissions take effect for a Kubernetes service account, the cluster must be created or updated with the option --no-enable-legacy-authorization. This requirement is removed for clusters running GKE version 1.8 or higher.
input_row.impact: Once the cluster has the legacy authorizer disabled, the user must be granted the ability to create authorization roles using RBAC to ensure that the role-based access control permissions take effect.
input_row.references: 1. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/role-based-access- control 2. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/hardening-your- cluster#leave_abac_disabled_default_for_110 Additional Information: On clusters running GKE 1.6 or 1.7, Kubernetes Service accounts have full permissions on the Kubernetes API by default. To ensure that the role-based access control permissions take effect for a Kubernetes service account, the cluster must be created or updated with the option --no-enable-legacy-authorization. This requirement is removed for clusters running GKE version 1.8 or higher.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
Given the detailed audit field instructions, the property for verifying if Legacy Authorization (ABAC) is disabled on a GKE cluster can be queried directly using Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) commands, making an automated audit feasible. The specific command provided by the CIS benchmark indicates that checking the `legacyAbac` field is accessible via the `gcloud container clusters describe` command. Thus, the control aligns well with an automated approach.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_gke_cluster_legacy_authorization_disabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- **GCP API calls or Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) SDK methods to use:**
  Utilize `gcloud container clusters describe <cluster_name> --zone <compute_zone> --format json` to query the cluster configuration.

- **GCP CLI commands:**
  Use the provided command: `gcloud container clusters describe <cluster_name> --zone <compute_zone> --format json | jq '.legacyAbac'`.

- **Specific resources to enumerate:**
  Enumerate through all Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) clusters available within a given project and across all regions or zones where the clusters are deployed.

- **Exact configuration properties/settings to validate:**
  Check the `legacyAbac` field in the cluster's JSON description. The expected secure configuration should return `null` or `{}` to indicate that Legacy Authorization is disabled.

- **Clear Pass/Fail criteria with expected values:**
  - **Pass:** The `legacyAbac` field should return `null` or `{}`, indicating Legacy Authorization is disabled.
  - **Fail:** If the `legacyAbac` field returns `true`, it indicates that Legacy Authorization is still enabled, failing the compliance check.

By automating this check, an organization can consistently ensure that ABAC is disabled across all applicable GKE clusters, adhering to recommended security practices.
